note_data.title: Data representation
keyPoints: **Binary** uses only two digits: 0 and 1, representing ON and OFF states in computer circuits
**Hexadecimal** uses base 16 (0-9, A-F) and is used as a shorthand for binary because one hex digit represents exactly 4 binary digits
**Two's complement** is used to represent negative numbers in binary by inverting all bits and adding 1
**ASCII** assigns a unique 7-bit binary code to each character, allowing 128 different characters
**Unicode** uses 16 or 32 bits per character, allowing representation of characters from all world languages including Urdu
**Bitmap images** are stored as pixels with each pixel having a binary value representing its colour
**Sound** is stored digitally by taking samples of the analogue sound wave at regular intervals and converting to binary
**Data compression** reduces file size by removing redundant or unnecessary data using lossless or lossy methods
**Storage capacity** is measured in bytes, with 1 byte = 8 bits, 1 KB = 1024 bytes, 1 MB = 1024 KB

definitions.definition: A number system that uses only two digits (0 and 1) to represent all data in computers
A number system using base 16 with digits 0-9 and letters A-F, used as shorthand for binary
A method for representing negative numbers in binary by inverting all bits and adding 1
American Standard Code for Information Interchange - a 7-bit character encoding standard
The process of reducing the size of data files by removing redundant or unnecessary information

howToAnswer.instruction: Show your working step by step. For binary to denary: multiply each digit by its place value. For denary to binary: repeatedly divide by 2.
Give the method AND the reason why it works using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give the fact directly with no explanation or working

modelAnswer.question: Explain how the two's complement method is used to represent the denary number -6 in 8-bit binary.
modelAnswer.answer: First convert +6 to 8-bit binary: 00000110. Then invert all the bits: 11111001. Finally add 1 to get the two's complement: 11111010. This method works because it allows negative numbers to be stored using the same binary arithmetic operations as positive numbers.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Add 1 to get final answer (11111010)
Mark 4: Explain why method is used

commonMistakes: Never say 'flip the bits' — always say 'invert all the bits' when describing two's complement
Students often forget that 1 KB = 1024 bytes, not 1000 bytes
Never confuse ASCII with Unicode — ASCII is 7-bit, Unicode is 16 or 32-bit
Students often mix up lossless and lossy compression — lossless keeps all original data, lossy removes some data permanently
When converting binary to denary, always show place values (128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1) for 8-bit
examinerTips: Binary conversion questions appear in every paper — practice until automatic
Hexadecimal questions often ask for conversion to/from binary — remember 4 bits = 1 hex digit
Sound and image representation questions always ask about factors affecting quality and file size
Two's complement questions are common — always show all three steps for full marks
formulas: Sound file size = Sample rate × Sample resolution × Length × Number of channels
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Binary place value diagram showing 8-bit positions with values 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1 from left to right
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to convert hexadecimal pairs to 8-bit binary values, requiring knowledge of hex-to-binary conversion
This topic was tested in O/N 2022 P1 Q7 (2 marks) — they asked students to give the denary value for a 12-bit binary number, testing binary-to-decimal conversion
This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (3 marks) — they asked students to describe how text is converted to binary using character sets like ASCII/Unicode
This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P1 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to explain why programmers use hexadecimal representation instead of binary
This topic was tested in M/J 2021 P2 Q2 (2 marks) — they asked students to identify similarities between different number systems

workedExample.question: Convert the hexadecimal value A7 to an 8-bit binary value. (2 marks)
steps: Step 1: Split the hexadecimal into individual digits: A and 7
Step 2: Convert A to binary: A = 10 in denary = 1010 in 4-bit binary
Step 3: Convert 7 to binary: 7 in denary = 0111 in 4-bit binary
Step 5: Verify it's 8-bit (it is) and write final answer
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct method showing hex digits converted separately to 4-bit binary, 1 mark for correct final 8-bit binary answer

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to pad binary numbers to the correct number of bits' — always ensure your binary answer has the required number of bits (e.g. 8-bit must have exactly 8 digits)

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often confused the roles of ASCII and Unicode' — remember that both are character sets that assign unique binary codes to characters, but Unicode can represent many more characters

Examiner Report 2023: 'When explaining hexadecimal benefits, candidates gave vague answers like easier' — be specific: state 'easier to read/write', 'less prone to errors', 'shorter representation' or 'easier to debug'
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a number system conversion diagram showing the relationships between Binary (base 2), Denary (base 10), and Hexadecimal (base 16). The diagram should show: Binary using only 0s and 1s with place values as powers of 2 (128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1 for 8-bit), Denary using digits 0-9 with place values as powers of 10, and Hexadecimal using 0-9 and A-F with place values as powers of 16. Include conversion arrows between all three systems and show that each hex digit represents exactly 4 binary bits.
note_data.quickRecap: Data representation covers three number systems: binary (0,1), denary (0-9), and hexadecimal (0-F). Each hex digit equals 4 binary bits, making hex a shorthand for binary. Text is stored using character sets like ASCII/Unicode that assign unique binary codes to each character. Always pad binary answers to the required bit length and be specific when explaining why programmers use hex over binary.
